BiomasBR · PRODES-AMZ Project

SITS-AMZ Pipeline

End-to-end deforestation and degradation mapping for the Brazilian Amazon

8 Scripts SITS ≥ 1.5.4 R ≥ 4.3 BDC · Sentinel-2-16D Random Forest OBIA · Vector Cube 13 Classes

This book documents the complete processing pipeline of the PRODES-AMZ project — an application of the sits R package (v1.5.4) for annual deforestation and degradation monitoring in the Brazilian Legal Amazon. The pipeline runs on Sentinel-2 16-day composites from the Brazil Data Cube (BDC), covering tiles 012014, 012015, 013014, 013015 for the 2023-08–2025-07 reference period.

The pipeline follows a time-first, space-later paradigm: each spatial unit is associated with a multivariate time series of spectral bands, a machine learning model classifies those time series, and spatial post-processing rules refine the map. Unlike standard SITS workflows, classification is performed at segment level (Object-Based Image Analysis) using vector data cubes — producing results compatible with INPE's PRODES polygon-based outputs.

📖 Note

All scripts are available at github.com/migualex/sits-prodes. For theoretical background, refer to the SITS Book (Camara et al., 2024).

0.1Pipeline Overview

#ScriptDescription
011_SITS_mixture_model.RGenerate LSMM fraction bands — Soil, Veg, Water
022_SITS_segmentation.RSegment LSMM cube with SNIC → vector data cube
033_SITS_samples_quality_assessment.RExtract time series, 3× SOM QC, balance classes
044_SITS_model_training.RK-fold cross-validation, train RF (100 trees)
055_SITS_classification.RClassify vector cube, label map + entropy uncertainty
066_SITS_validation_samples_stratification.RRasterize map, stratified sample design + reclassification
077_SITS_validation_accuracy_measurement.RArea-adjusted accuracy — full map + PRODES-Degradation
088_SITS_post_processing.RPRODES mask, 1 ha MMU, spatial adjacency rule

0.2Classification Schema (13 Classes)

Corpo_Dagua
Corte_Raso_Com_Arvores_Remanescentes
Corte_Raso
Corte_Raso_Antigo
Corte_Raso_Com_Fogo
Corte_Raso_Com_Vegetacao
Corte_Raso_Antigo_Com_Vegetacao
Degradacao
Degradacao_Por_Fogo
Floresta
Floresta_Transicional
Vegetacao_Natural_Nao_Florestal
Area_Inundavel
Next →Introduction
Chapter 1

Introduction

Context, paradigm, and key concepts

1.1PRODES and BiomasBR

The PRODES program, operated by INPE, has continuously mapped deforestation in the Brazilian Legal Amazon since 1988. PRODES tracks gross annual deforestation — primary forest removed within each 12-month reference year (August–July) — using Landsat-class imagery and polygon-based shapefiles with a 1 ha minimum mapping unit. This pipeline extends PRODES compatible mapping to include forest degradation classes (Degradacao, Degradacao_Por_Fogo) alongside the standard deforestation classes, using Sentinel-2 time series and OBIA classification.

1.2Time-First, Space-Later

The sits package follows a time-first, space-later paradigm (SITS Book, Introduction). In the time-first phase, each pixel or segment is represented as a multivariate time series across all acquisition dates. A machine learning model classifies these time series. In the space-later phase, spatial post-processing rules refine the map. This captures seasonal phenological patterns — e.g., the NDVI cycle of pasture vs. native vegetation — that single-date composites cannot discriminate.

1.3Object-Based Image Analysis with Vector Data Cubes

Rather than classifying individual pixels, this pipeline segments the image into spatially homogeneous objects (SNIC superpixels), assembles them into a vector data cube (SITS Book, Ch. 25), and classifies the time series extracted per segment. This eliminates salt-and-pepper noise, produces polygon outputs compatible with the PRODES data model, and allows setting a minimum mapping unit before classification by controlling segment size.

1.4Brazil Data Cube as Data Source

All imagery is sourced from the Brazil Data Cube (BDC) collection SENTINEL-2-16D — analysis-ready 16-day best-pixel composites at 10 m resolution. The BDC applies cloud masking and compositing, making the cube directly suitable for time series analysis without additional preprocessing. Bands used: B02, B03, B04, B05, B06, B07, B08, B8A, B11, B12, NDVI, NBR, EVI, CLOUD.

← PreviousPreface Next →Setup
Chapter 2

Setup

Installation, dependencies, and project structure

2.1Installation

R
install.packages("sits")     # version ≥ 1.5.4
install.packages(c("sf", "terra", "dplyr", "tibble", "ggplot2",
                   "lubridate", "purrr", "stringr", "fs",
                   "RColorBrewer", "gdalUtilities", "pals", "cli"))
⚠ BDC Access Token

All cubes are sourced from the Brazil Data Cube (source = "BDC"). A free BDC access token is required. Register at brazildatacube.org and configure with sits_config(bdc_access_key = "...").

2.2Project Structure

bash
prodes.amz/
├── scripts/                         # 8 numbered R scripts
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   │   ├── mixture_model/           # LSMM fraction tiles (SOIL, VEG, WATER)
│   │   ├── samples/                 # training (.gpkg) + validation samples
│   │   │   └── validation_samples/  # stratified validation points
│   │   └── auxiliary/               # PRODES mask, BDC CRS file
│   ├── segments/                    # SNIC segmented vector cubes (.gpkg)
│   ├── rds/
│   │   ├── time_series/             # extracted & cleaned time series (.rds)
│   │   ├── som/                     # SOM maps (.rds)
│   │   └── model/random_forest/     # trained RF model (.rds)
│   ├── class/                       # classified maps (.gpkg)
│   │   ├── raster/                  # rasterized class maps (.tif)
│   │   └── post_process/            # final post-processed output (.gpkg)
│   └── plots/                       # SOM / accuracy / area plots (.png)
← PreviousIntroduction Next →Step 1 — LSMM
Chapter 3 · Step 1

Spectral Mixture Model (LSMM)

Decompose pixels into Soil, Vegetation, and Water fraction bands

01 · 1_SITS_mixture_model.R

sits_mixture_model() (SITS Book, Ch. 9) decomposes each pixel's reflectance spectrum into proportions of three pure spectral endmembers using non-negative least squares (NNLS). This pipeline uses Soil, Vegetation, and Water endmembers — band values taken from Small & Sousa (2022) for Sentinel-2 MSI. The resulting fraction bands (0–10 000 scale) enrich the feature space used for classification, improving separability in mixed transition zones common in the Amazon.

Input
BDC SENTINEL-2-16D cube
Tiles: 012014, 012015, 013014, 013015
2023-08-01 → 2025-07-31
Output
LSMM cube → data/raw/mixture_model/
Fraction bands: SOIL, VEG, WATER

3.1Code

1_SITS_mixture_model.R
# Load Required Libraries
library(sits)
library(tibble)

# Step 1 -- Define and Load Raster Data Cube from BDC collection
cube <- sits_cube(
  source     = "BDC",
  collection = "SENTINEL-2-16D",
  bands      = c('B02', 'B03', 'B04', 'B05', 'B06', 'B07', 'B08', 'B8A', 'B11', 'B12'),
  tiles      = c('012014', '012015', '013014', '013015'),
  start_date = '2023-08-01',
  end_date   = '2025-07-31',
  progress   = TRUE)

# Step 2 -- Define endmember spectra
# Values from: Small & Sousa (2022) Remote Sens. 14, 5748
endmembers <- tibble::tribble(
  ~class,   ~B02,  ~B03,  ~B04,  ~B05,  ~B06,  ~B07,  ~B08,  ~B8A,  ~B11,  ~B12,
  "soil",  1799, 2154, 3028, 3303, 3472, 3656, 3566, 3686, 5097, 4736,
  "veg",    827,  892,  410, 1070, 4206, 5646, 5495, 6236, 2101,  775,
  "water",  946,  739,  280,  208,  180,  167,  135,  129,   26,   14,
)

# Step 3 -- Generate LSMM fraction cube
mm_cube <- sits_mixture_model(
  data       = cube,
  endmembers = endmembers,
  multicores = 28,  # adapt to your CPU
  memsize    = 180, # adapt to your RAM
  output_dir = "data/raw/mixture_model"
)
💡 Endmember values

Band values are in BDC native scale (0–10000). Values from Small & Sousa (2022) were validated for the Sentinel-2 MSI spectral mixing space and are widely used for tropical land cover monitoring.

← PreviousSetup Next →Step 2 — Segmentation
Chapter 4 · Step 2

Segmentation (OBIA)

Partition the LSMM cube into spatially homogeneous objects

02 · 2_SITS_segmentation.R

sits_segment() (SITS Book, Ch. 25) applies SNIC (Simple Non-Iterative Clustering) to the LSMM fraction bands. Three parameters control output: spacing (target segment radius in pixels), compactness (trade-off between spectral and spatial homogeneity), and padding (border pixel handling). The version string encodes all parameters automatically, ensuring reproducibility. Only one tile (012014) is processed per run; repeat for each tile.

Input
LSMM cube from data/raw/mixture_model/
Bands: SOIL, VEG, WATER · Tile: 012014
Output
Segmented vector cube → data/segments/
Version-encoded filenames (.gpkg)

4.1Code

2_SITS_segmentation.R
# Step 1 -- Load LSMM cube (SOIL, VEG, WATER fraction bands)
mm_cube_fraction_features <- sits_cube(
  source     = "BDC",
  collection = "SENTINEL-2-16D",
  tiles      = "012014",
  data_dir   = "data/raw/mixture_model",
  bands      = c("SOIL", "VEG", "WATER"),
  start_date = '2024-08-01',
  end_date   = '2025-07-31')

# Step 2 -- SNIC segmentation parameters
spacing      <- 10
compactness  <- 0.5
padding      <- 0
grid_seeding <- "rectangular"

# Version string encodes all parameters for reproducibility
version <- paste0("LSMM-SNIC-spac", spacing, "-comp",
                   gsub("\\\\.", "", as.character(compactness)),
                   "-pad", padding, "-", grid_seeding, "-",
                   format(Sys.Date(), "%Y-%m-%d"))

# Step 3 -- Segment the LSMM cube with SNIC
mm_cube_segments <- sits_segment(
  cube       = mm_cube_fraction_features,
  seg_fn     = sits_snic(
    grid_seeding = grid_seeding,
    spacing      = spacing,
    compactness  = compactness,
    padding      = padding
  ),
  memsize    = 180,
  multicores = 28,
  output_dir = "data/segments",
  version    = version
)
💡 Parameter tuning

spacing = 10 at 10 m resolution targets ~100 m² segments. compactness = 0.5 allows elongated shapes that follow land cover boundaries. grid_seeding = "rectangular" places seed pixels on a regular grid, which is more reproducible than random seeding.

← PreviousStep 1 — LSMM Next →Step 3 — Sample QA
Chapter 5 · Step 3

Sample Quality Assessment

3× SOM quality control, noise removal, and class balancing

03 · 3_SITS_samples_quality_assessment.R

Training samples are loaded from amostras_amazonia_24_02_2026.gpkg. Time series are extracted at n_sam_pol = 16 internal points per polygon with pol_avg = FALSE (individual points, not the polygon mean), maximising within-class spectral variability. Quality control uses SOM (SITS Book, Ch. 14) with DTW distance — more robust to temporal distortions than Euclidean distance for time series comparison.

Thresholds are stricter than defaults: prior ≥ 0.80 and posterior ≥ 0.70. The rare class DESMAT_DEGRAD_FOGO is always retained regardless of SOM score to prevent it from being eliminated by strict filtering. Imbalance reduction targets 300–500 samples per class.

Input
Merged S2 + LSMM cube (4 tiles)
Training polygons: amostras_amazonia_24_02_2026.gpkg
Output
Cleaned & balanced TS → data/rds/time_series/
SOM maps & QA plots → data/rds/som/ + data/plots/

5.1Code

3_SITS_samples_quality_assessment.R
# Step 1 -- Merge Sentinel-2 cube with LSMM fraction cube
cube_merge <- sits_merge(mm_cube, cube)

# Step 2 -- Extract time series from training samples
#  n_sam_pol=16 : 16 internal points sampled per polygon
#  pol_avg=FALSE: keep individual time series (not polygon average)
samples <- sits_get_data(
  cube      = cube_merge,
  samples   = sf::st_read("data/raw/samples/amostras_amazonia_24_02_2026.gpkg"),
  n_sam_pol = 16,
  pol_avg   = FALSE,
  label     = "label",
  multicores = 1,
  progress  = TRUE)

# ── SOM Round 1: initial quality screening ─────────────────────
som_cluster <- sits_som_map(
  samples,
  grid_xdim = 2,  # start 2×2, then tune to SITS recommendation
  grid_ydim = 2,
  alpha     = 1.0,
  distance  = "dtw",
  rlen      = 20)
som_eval <- sits_som_evaluate_cluster(som_cluster)

# Step 3 -- Clean samples: prior ≥ 0.80, posterior ≥ 0.70
all_samples <- sits_som_clean_samples(
  som_map              = som_cluster,
  prior_threshold      = 0.80,
  posterior_threshold  = 0.70,
  keep                 = c("clean", "analyze", "remove"))

# Manually keep rare class despite low score
clean_samples <- all_samples %>%
  filter(eval == "clean" | label == "DESMAT_DEGRAD_FOGO")

# ── SOM Round 2: confirm clean set ─────────────────────────────
som_cluster_clean <- sits_som_map(all_samples, grid_xdim = 2, grid_ydim = 2,
                                    alpha = 1.0, distance = "dtw", rlen = 20)

# ── Balance classes ─────────────────────────────────────────────
clean_samples_balanced <- sits_reduce_imbalance(
  samples         = clean_samples,
  n_samples_over  = 300,
  n_samples_under = 500)

# Remove columns with NA values before saving
clean_samples_balanced <- clean_samples_balanced[, colSums(is.na(clean_samples_balanced)) == 0]

# ── SOM Round 3: validate balanced set ─────────────────────────
som_cluster_clean_balanced <- sits_som_map(clean_samples_balanced, grid_xdim = 2, grid_ydim = 2,
                                             alpha = 1.0, distance = "dtw", rlen = 20)
som_eval_clean_balanced <- sits_som_evaluate_cluster(som_cluster_clean_balanced)
📝 Starting grid size

Always start with grid_xdim = 2, grid_ydim = 2. Run once and inspect the output: sits prints a recommended grid dimension range based on sample count. Then re-run with the recommended value for the final SOM.

← PreviousStep 2 — Segmentation Next →Step 4 — Training
Chapter 6 · Step 4

Model Training

K-fold cross-validation and Random Forest training

04 · 4_SITS_model_training.R

The script uses sits_rfor() — the Random Forest implementation in sits — with num_trees = 100 for the final model (after cross-validation with folds = 5). set.seed(88) ensures reproducibility. The script also exports the model via sits_model_export() to inspect OOB error curves by number of trees, verifying that 100 trees is sufficient for convergence.

⚠ Cross-validation ≠ Map accuracy

K-fold cross-validation measures model performance on training samples, not map accuracy. Training samples are not a probability sample of the landscape. For map accuracy, see Scripts 6 and 7.

Input
Cleaned & balanced TS from data/rds/time_series/
Output
Trained RF model → data/rds/model/random_forest/
Plots: confusion matrix, metrics, OOB curve

6.1Code

4_SITS_model_training.R
# Step 1 -- Load cleaned & balanced time series
train_samples <- readRDS("data/rds/time_series/samples_4-tiles-..._all_samples_new_....rds")

# Step 2 -- K-fold cross-validation (5 folds, sits_rfor)
set.seed(88)
rfor_validate <- sits_kfold_validate(
  samples    = train_samples,
  folds      = 5,
  ml_method  = sits_rfor(),  # randomForest with default params
  multicores = 28,
  progress   = TRUE)

# Visualise results
plot(rfor_validate, type = "confusion_matrix")
plot(rfor_validate, type = "metrics")

# Step 3 -- Train final model (100 trees)
rf_model <- sits_train(
  samples   = train_samples,
  ml_method = sits_rfor(num_trees = 100)
)

# Step 4 -- Inspect variable importance & OOB error by ntree
plot(rf_model)
rf_model2 <- sits_model_export(rf_model)
matplot(rf_model2$err.rate, type = "l", lty = 1, lwd = 2,
        main = "Out of Box error by the number of trees",
        xlab = "Number of Trees (ntree)", ylab = "OOB Error")

# Step 5 -- Save trained model
saveRDS(rf_model, "data/rds/model/random_forest/RF-model_....rds")
← PreviousStep 3 — Sample QA Next →Step 5 — Classification
Chapter 7 · Step 5

Classification

Classify vector data cube and compute entropy uncertainty

05 · 5_SITS_classification.R

sits_classify() applies the trained RF to the vector data cube at n_sam_pol = 16 — the same sampling density used during training, ensuring consistent feature extraction. sits_label_classification() assigns the majority-probability class to each segment. The uncertainty step rasterizes entropy values, scales by ×10 000 to UInt16, and saves with DEFLATE compression. This raster is used directly in the validation stratification (Script 6).

Input
Segmented vector cube (segments/)
Merged S2 + LSMM cube · RF model
Output
*_probs_*.gpkg — per-class probabilities
*_class_*.gpkg — final label map
*_entropy_*_raster.tif — uncertainty

7.1Code

5_SITS_classification.R
# Step 1 -- Load merged classification cube (S2 + LSMM)
cube_merge_lsmm_class <- sits_merge(mm_cube, cube)

# Step 2 -- Load local segmented vector cube
local_segs_cube <- sits_cube(
  source      = "BDC",
  collection  = "SENTINEL-2-16D",
  raster_cube = cube_merge_lsmm_class,
  vector_dir  = "data/segments",
  vector_band = "segments",
  version     = seg_version,
  parse_info  = c("satellite", "sensor", "tile",
                  "start_date", "end_date", "band", "version"))

# Step 3 -- Classify segments
#  n_sam_pol=16: 16 time series per segment, same as training
rf_model <- readRDS("data/rds/model/random_forest/RF-model_....rds")

class_prob <- sits_classify(
  data        = local_segs_cube,
  ml_model    = rf_model,
  multicores  = 28,
  memsize     = 180,
  output_dir  = tile_period_dir,
  version     = version,
  n_sam_pol   = 16,
  progress    = TRUE)

# Step 4 -- Generate final class label map
class_map <- sits_label_classification(
  cube       = class_prob,
  output_dir = tile_period_dir,
  version    = version,
  multicores = 28,
  progress   = TRUE)

# Step 5 -- Entropy uncertainty → rasterize to GeoTIFF (UInt16)
uncertainty <- sits_uncertainty(
  vector_cube, type = "entropy",
  multicores = 28, memsize = 180,
  output_dir = tile_period_dir, version = version, progress = TRUE)

# Rasterize entropy polygons, scale ×10000, save compressed GeoTIFF
uncertainty_raster_uint16 <- round(rasterize(uncertainty_polygons,
                                              raster_template, field = "entropy") * 10000)
writeRaster(uncertainty_raster_uint16,
            filename = file.path(tile_period_dir, "entropy_raster.tif"),
            datatype = "INT2U",
            gdal     = c("COMPRESS=DEFLATE", "PREDICTOR=2", "ZLEVEL=9"))
💡 Active learning with entropy

Segments with high entropy (near max for 13 classes ≈ 3.7 nats) are most informative for active learning. Adding their labels to the training set improves subsequent model iterations most efficiently.

← PreviousStep 4 — Training Next →Step 6 — Validation Stratification
Chapter 8 · Step 6

Validation — Sample Stratification

Rasterize map, design stratified sampling, and reclassify for PRODES compatibility

06 · 6_SITS_validation_samples_stratification.R

This script performs two distinct tasks. First, it rasterizes the classified vector map to a 10 m GeoTIFF using a custom sits_rasterize_segments() function (based on gdal_rasterize), which assigns integer class codes matching the trained model's label order and generates a QGIS-compatible QML style file. Second, it generates two sets of stratified validation samples: one for the full 13-class map and one for a 3-class PRODES-Degradation reclassification (Deforestation / Degradation / Other_Classes).

The sampling design uses std_err = 0.01 (overall accuracy SE ≤ 1%) with class-specific target user's accuracies and rare_class_prop = 0.025 to guarantee minimum sample sizes for rare classes. The overhead = 1.2 parameter generates 20% extra samples to compensate for border and cloud-contaminated pixels that will be discarded during labeling.

Input
Classified vector map (*_class_*.gpkg)
RF model (for label order)
PRODES mask (for reclassification)
Output
Rasterized class map → data/class/raster/
Validation samples (.gpkg) → data/raw/samples/validation_samples/

8.1Code

6_SITS_validation_samples_stratification.R
# Step 1 -- Rasterize classified vector segments to GeoTIFF
#  Uses sits_rasterize_segments() (custom function in the script)
raster_files <- purrr::map(class_files, function(file) {
  sits_rasterize_segments(file = file, res = 10,
                           style = style,
                           class_raster_dir = tile_period_dir)
})

# Step 2 -- Full-map stratified sampling design
#  expected_ua: target user's accuracy per class
#  std_err=0.01: overall accuracy SE ≤ 1%
#  rare_class_prop=0.025: floor for small classes
sampling_design <- sits_sampling_design(
  cube        = cube,
  expected_ua = c(
    "Floresta"           = 0.95,
    "Corte_Raso"         = 0.70,
    "Degradacao"         = 0.70,
    "Corpo_Dagua"        = 0.95,
    "Area_Inundavel"     = 0.70
    # ... all 11 classes
  ),
  alloc_options  = c(120, 100, 75, 50, 30),
  std_err        = 0.01,
  rare_class_prop = 0.025)

# Step 3 -- Draw stratified random samples
samples_sf <- sits_stratified_sampling(
  cube            = cube,
  sampling_design = sampling_design,
  alloc           = "alloc_30",
  overhead        = 1.2,  # 20% overhead for border/cloud pixels
  progress        = TRUE,
  multicores      = 12)

sf::st_write(samples_sf, "data/raw/samples/validation_samples/samples-validation-full-map_....gpkg")

# ── PRODES-Degradation reclassification ─────────────────────────
# Reduces 13 classes to 3 aggregated classes before PRODES-style accuracy
cube_reclass <- sits_reclassify(
  cube  = cube,
  mask  = prodes_mask,
  rules = list(
    "Deforestation" = cube %in% c("Corte_Raso_Com_Arvores_Remanescentes",
                                   "Corte_Raso", "Corte_Raso_Com_Vegetacao"),
    "Degradation"   = cube %in% c("Degradacao", "Degradacao_Por_Fogo"),
    "Other_Classes" = cube %in% c("Floresta", "Corpo_Dagua", /* ... */)
  ),
  version    = "prodes-degradation",
  output_dir = dir_path,
  multicores = 24, memsize = 180)
⚠ Independent labeling required

After this script, validation sample points must be labeled by an independent analyst using high-resolution reference imagery (Planet, Google Earth) from the same reference period. Never reuse training labels and never use the classified map itself as reference data.

← PreviousStep 5 — Classification Next →Step 7 — Accuracy
Chapter 9 · Step 7

Validation — Accuracy Assessment

Area-adjusted accuracy for full map and PRODES-Degradation classes

07 · 7_SITS_validation_accuracy_measurement.R

sits_accuracy() (SITS Book, Ch. 24) implements the design-based area-adjusted framework of Olofsson et al. (2014). Because mapped area proportions are biased estimators of true area in the presence of classification error, the estimator weights confusion matrix entries by the mapped area of each stratum. This yields unbiased area estimates with confidence intervals.

The script runs accuracy assessment twice: once for the full 13-class map and once for the 3-class PRODES-Degradation reclassification (Deforestation / Degradation / Other_Classes). Each assessment produces a confusion matrix, per-class user's and producer's accuracies, error-adjusted area estimates in hectares, and 95% confidence intervals — all exported as plots via ggplot2.

Input
Classified raster cube (data/class/raster/)
Labeled validation samples (.gpkg)
Output
Confusion matrix · User's/Producer's accuracy
Error-adjusted area estimates ± 95% CI
Plots → data/plots/

9.1Code

7_SITS_validation_accuracy_measurement.R
# Step 1 -- Load classified raster cube
cube <- sits_cube(
  source     = "BDC",
  collection = "SENTINEL-2-16D",
  bands      = "class",
  labels     = labels,       # from sits_labels(model)
  data_dir   = cube_dirs,    # classified raster folder
  version    = version)

# Step 2 -- Full-map accuracy (13 classes)
samples_validation <- sf::st_read(grep("*full-map*", samples_validation_list, value = TRUE))

area_acc_full_map <- sits_accuracy(
  cube       = cube,
  validation = samples_validation,
  memsize    = 180,
  multicores = 28)

area_acc_full_map$error_matrix           # confusion matrix
area_acc_full_map$accuracy               # user's & producer's accuracy
area_acc_full_map$error_ajusted_area     # error-adjusted area (ha)
area_acc_full_map$conf_interval          # 95% CI per class

# Step 3 -- PRODES-Degradation accuracy (3 aggregated classes)
samples_prodes <- sf::st_read(grep("*desmat-degrad*", samples_validation_list, value = TRUE))

area_acc_prodes <- sits_accuracy(
  cube       = cube_reclass,
  validation = samples_prodes,
  memsize    = 180,
  multicores = 28)

area_acc_prodes$accuracy    # Deforestation / Degradation / Other_Classes
area_acc_prodes$error_ajusted_area  # area estimates ± CI
← PreviousStep 6 — Stratification Next →Step 8 — Post-Processing
Chapter 10 · Step 8

Post-Processing

PRODES mask, minimum mapping unit, and spatial adjacency rules

08 · 8_SITS_post_processing.R

Five sequential spatial operations produce a PRODES-compatible deforestation layer. The pipeline applies the PRODES historical mask (st_difference()) to retain only new deforestation not covered by prior PRODES years, enforces the 1 ha minimum mapping unit, and applies a class-specific spatial adjacency rule: polygons of class DESMAT_ARVORE_REMANESCE (deforestation with remnant trees) are retained only if they are spatially adjacent to other deforestation classes and have area ≥ 6.25 ha — a stricter MMU for this ambiguous class. All processing is done in SIRGAS 2000 / Albers Equal-Area projection.

Input
Classified map: *_class_*.gpkg
PRODES mask: grade_bdc_acumulado_24_10857.gpkg
Output
Final layer → data/class/post_process/
Classes: 4 deforestation types
Attributes: class, area_ha

10.1Code

8_SITS_post_processing.R
# INPUT PARAMETERS
class_obj           <- "SENTINEL-2_MSI_012014_2024-07-27_2025-07-28_class_1y-012014"
classification_path <- paste0("data/class/", class_obj, ".gpkg")
prodes_mask_path    <- "data/raw/auxiliary/grade_bdc_acumulado_24_10857.gpkg"
output_path         <- paste0("data/class/post_process/", class_obj, "_post-processed.gpkg")
crs_albers          <- "+proj=aea +lat_0=-12 +lon_0=-54 ..."  # SIRGAS 2000 / Albers

# Step 1 -- Load & filter deforestation classes
classification <- sf::read_sf(classification_path)
deforestation_classes <- c("DESMAT_ARVORE_REMANESCE", "DESMAT_CORTE_RASO",
                              "DESMAT_DEGRAD_FOGO",     "DESMAT_VEG")
classification_filtered <- classification %>%
  filter(class %in% deforestation_classes) %>%
  st_transform(crs_albers)

# Step 2 -- Dissolve by class, explode to singlepart polygons
classification_dissolved <- classification_filtered %>%
  group_by(class) %>%
  summarise(geom = st_union(geom)) %>%
  st_cast("MULTIPOLYGON") %>%
  st_cast("POLYGON")  # disaggregate multipart

# Step 3 -- Apply PRODES historical mask (st_difference)
mask_tile  <- prodes_mask %>% filter(tile == !!tile) %>% st_transform(crs_albers)
difference <- st_difference(classification_dissolved, st_make_valid(mask_tile)) %>%
  st_make_valid() %>% st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>% st_cast("POLYGON")

# Step 4 -- Minimum mapping unit: 1 ha
result_filtered <- difference %>%
  mutate(area_ha = as.numeric(st_area(geom)) / 10000) %>%
  filter(!st_is_empty(.) , area_ha >= 1)

# Step 5 -- Spatial rule: DESMAT_ARVORE_REMANESCE must touch other deforestation
#           AND have area ≥ 6.25 ha
arvore_remanesce <- result_filtered %>% filter(class == "DESMAT_ARVORE_REMANESCE")
outras_classes   <- result_filtered %>% filter(class != "DESMAT_ARVORE_REMANESCE")

# Keep only non-disjoint remnant polygons (touching other deforestation)
is_separated     <- st_disjoint(arvore_remanesce, st_union(outras_classes), sparse = FALSE)[, 1]
arvore_remanesce <- arvore_remanesce[!is_separated, ] %>% filter(area_ha >= 6.25)

final_result <- dplyr::bind_rows(outras_classes, arvore_remanesce)
sf::st_write(final_result, output_path, delete_dsn = TRUE)
📝 PRODES compatibility

The output uses SIRGAS 2000 / Albers projection, 1 ha MMU (6.25 ha for remnant-tree class), and the same 4-class deforestation schema as PRODES. Report area statistics using the area-adjusted estimates from Script 7, not raw polygon areas.
